entry.title: open-gpu-kernel-modules compilation
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-18

init

developer.nvidia.com

https://developer.nvidia.com/

Source code

open-gpu-kernel-modules

https://github.com/NVIDIA/open-gpu-kernel-modules

cross toolchain

This article uses Arm GNU Toolchain 11.2-2022.02（aarch64-none-linux-gnu- triplet).

ARM has released a total of 8 architectures: ARMv1, ARMv2, ARMv3, ARMv4, ARMv5, ARMv6, ARMv7, ARMv8.

For processors supporting the ARMv8 instruction set, you can use-march=armv8-aparameter to compile code. The ARM GNU compiler can be downloaded from the link below

Arm GNU Toolchain Downloads

https://developer.arm.com/downloads/-/arm-gnu-toolchain-downloads

It is recommended to use the compiler from the ARM official website, which is portable and can be used after extraction.

This article uses the 11.2 version of the cross-compiler

aarch64-none-linux-gnu 11.2

https://developer.arm.com/-/media/files/downloads/gnu/11.2-2022.02/binrel/gcc-arm-11.2-2022.02-x86_64-aarch64-none-linux-gnu.tar.xz

aarch64-none-linux-gnu 11.2

https://pan.quark.cn/s/c5681aa0b3ef

Extraction code:71g1

You can add it to the environment variable

Using version 11.2.1 of aarch64-none-linux-gnu-gcc

If the toolchain triplet is aarch64-linux-gnu-(e.g., Ubuntu’s gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu package), replace all the following aarch64-none-linux-gnu- with aarch64-linux-gnu- That’s it.

Compilation

Key points:

  1. ARCH=arm64 for Kbuild, TARGET_ARCH=aarch64 for NVIDIA’s utils.mk (they cannot be mixed).
  2. The toolchain uses PATH + explicit CC/CXX/LD/AR as a double guarantee, because utils.mk does not read CROSS_COMPILE.
  3. SYSSRC/SYSOUT point to linux-5.10.238 configured with the same toolchain.

To clean:

Description

  1. Native compilation does not need CROSS_COMPILE, just use the local gcc. Still need to explicitly pass CC/CXX/LD/AR because utils.mk does not read CROSS_COMPILE。
  2. The difference from arm64 is only in ARCH/TARGET_ARCH: for arm64 it is ARCH=arm64 + TARGET_ARCH=aarch64；x86_for 64 both are x86_64. Note that utils.mk does not have a TARGET_ARCH called arm64, only aarch64.

Clean

Directory structure

1. Top-level directory structure

Core mechanism: two-layer build model

The key to understanding this repository lies in its two-layer build model. This repository is not a single Linux kernel module, but is assembled from the NVIDIA OS-independent core and the Linux kernel adaptation layer, ultimately generating 5 .ko modules.

  • First layer output: produced in src/nvidia/_out/Linux_<ARCH>/nv-kernel.o. Code uses -ffreestanding and -fno-stack-protector compiled, so that the core logic can be reused across different operating systems (such as Linux, FreeBSD, etc.).

  • Second layer: The .o files generated in the first layer are linked as ‘black-box binaries’ into the final .ko modules, and wrapped with Linux-specific hook code.

2. src: OS-independent core

(approximately 2.11 million lines)

3. src/nvidia/src/kernel/gpu/ Subdirectory (GPU IP engines)

This is the core hardware control part of the driver, divided by hardware engines:

  • gr/: Graphics (rasterization/shading/rendering core)
  • fifo/: Command submission queue management
  • ce/: Copy Engine (high-performance memory copy engine)
  • intr/: Interrupt handling
  • sec2/: Security engine
  • falcon/: Falcon microcontroller core framework
  • gsp/: ★ GSP (GPU System Processor) core
  • fsp/：Firmware Security Processor
  • gsplite/: Lightweight GSP logic
  • pmu/: Performance monitoring unit
  • nvdec/ nvenc/ nvjpg/ ofa/: Video codec and optical flow acceleration engines
  • bif/ bus/: PCIe bus interface and bus control
  • mem_mgr/ mem_sys/ mmu/ mig_mgr/: Video memory management, MMU page tables, MIG instance partitioning
  • disp/: Display output control
  • perf/ hwpm/ rc/ timer/ …: Performance, hardware monitoring, Reset recovery and timers

Architecture evolution background: The RM (Resource Manager) core of modern NVIDIA GPUs mainly runs on the GSP (a RISC-V microprocessor) inside the GPU. A large part of the code in src/nvidia is actually the source code of the GSP firmware itself—this is also the key underlying evolution of NVIDIA gradually moving towards open source after 2020.

4. kernel-open/ Linux adaptation layer

(approximately 220,000 lines)

conftest.sh Mechanism: This is the core secret of how the NVIDIA driver can be “one source code compatible with countless kernel versions”. Before compilation, it performs a large number of snippet compilation tests for the target kernel (e.g., detecting whether kmem exists_cache_create_usercopy、pgprot_t, etc.). The test results are written into conftest/headers.h, and then in the source code through #if NV_IS_ENABLED(...) macros to conditionally compile the corresponding compatibility code.

Final generated kernel modules

Module nameMain functionMain source code composition
nvidia.koMain driver core: Create /dev/nvidia* Device node, providing the core ioctl business entry.kernel-open/nvidia/ + src/nvidia/ Compiled nv-kernel.o
nvidia-modeset.koDisplay mode setting: Controls resolution, refresh rate, multi-screen display splicing, etc.kernel-open/nvidia-modeset/ + src/nvidia-modeset/ Compiled core .o
nvidia-drm.koDRM/KMS integration: Direct rendering manager adaptation required by modern Linux graphics stack (e.g., Wayland).kernel-open/nvidia-drm/
nvidia-uvm.koUnified virtual memory management: Implements unified CPU/GPU memory addressing. Core of HPC and AI computing, huge in size.kernel-open/nvidia-uvm/ (Approximately 150,000 lines of independent implementation)
nvidia-peermem.koRDMA P2P support: Allows third-party PCIe devices (e.g., Mellanox NICs) to directly read/write video memory via GPUDirect.kernel-open/nvidia-peermem/

Code size distribution

entry.title: OpenSSL 1.0.0s aarch64 cross compilation
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-16

init

This article describes how to compile OpenSSL 1.0.0s for aarch64 on x86_64 host using ARM cross toolchain to compile OpenSSL 1.0.0s aarch64 shared library complete process, including environment preparation, Configure configuration (need to use linux-aarch64 target and through environment variable CROSS_COMPILE specify toolchain prefix), modify ARFLAGS and AR definitions in three Makefiles to solve thin archive and parameter order issues, as well as compilation, installation and common problem troubleshooting methods.

Objective: On x86_64 host, use aarch64-none-linux-gnu- cross toolchain, compile libssl.so and libcrypto.so(ARM aarch64 shared library).

1. Environment preparation

1.1 Source code

If you don’t have to use openssl-1.0.0, openssl 1.1.1 is more recommended, not only the code is more robust, but also compilation is simpler.

1.2 Cross toolchain

This article uses Arm GNU Toolchain 11.2-2022.02（aarch64-none-linux-gnu- triplet).

ARM has released a total of 8 architectures: ARMv1, ARMv2, ARMv3, ARMv4, ARMv5, ARMv6, ARMv7, ARMv8.

For processors supporting the ARMv8 instruction set, you can use-march=armv8-aparameter to compile code. The ARM GNU compiler can be downloaded from the link below

Arm GNU Toolchain Downloads

https://developer.arm.com/downloads/-/arm-gnu-toolchain-downloads

It is recommended to use the compiler from the ARM official website, which is portable and can be used after extraction.

This article uses the 11.2 version of the cross-compiler

aarch64-none-linux-gnu 11.2

https://developer.arm.com/-/media/files/downloads/gnu/11.2-2022.02/binrel/gcc-arm-11.2-2022.02-x86_64-aarch64-none-linux-gnu.tar.xz

aarch64-none-linux-gnu 11.2

https://pan.quark.cn/s/c5681aa0b3ef

Extraction code:71g1

You can add it to the environment variable

Using version 11.2.1 of aarch64-none-linux-gnu-gcc

If the toolchain triplet is aarch64-linux-gnu-(e.g., Ubuntu’s gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu package), replace all the following aarch64-none-linux-gnu- with aarch64-linux-gnu- That’s it.

2. Configuration (Configure)

OpenSSL 1.0.0 does not linux-aarch64 This target (even if the command line can select it, it will fall back to the host’s cc, the output is x86_64).
The correct approach is to use the generic 64-bit target linux-generic64, and throughenvironment variable CROSS_COMPILE specify the toolchain prefix.

⚠️ Important：CROSS_COMPILE Must use environment variable pass in.
If written as ./Configure ... CROSS_COMPILE=... command line argument,
The Configure script of OpenSSL 1.0.0 will report:
target already defined - linux-generic64 (offending arg: CROSS_COMPILE=...)

After configuration is complete, check Makefile Confirm that cross-compilation is effective:

Expected output:

if CC= cc(no prefix), indicating that cross-compilation did not take effect, need to reconfigure.

2.1 Configure parameter description

./Configure The complete parameter meanings:

ParameterstypeDescription
linux-generic64targetGeneral 64-bit Linux platform. OpenSSL 1.0.0 does not have linux-aarch64 target, must use this
sharedoptionAlso build shared library (.so). If not added, only static library is generated (.a）
--prefix=$PWD/_installpathmake install installation root directory (headers, libraries, certificates, etc.)
--openssldir=$PWD/_install/sslpathDefault directory for OpenSSL to look for certificates/configuration files at runtime
no-ideaDisable algorithmIDEA symmetric encryption.Patented algorithm(patent has expired, but old versions of OpenSSL do not compile by default), not writing will report idea.h: No such file
no-md2Disable algorithmMD2 hash. Has been provenextremely insecure(collided as early as 1998), no need to keep
no-mdc2Disable algorithmMDC2 hash. Old algorithm, rarely used
no-rc5Disabled algorithmRC5 symmetric encryption.Patented algorithm(expired), not compiled by default
no-ssl2Disabled protocolSSL 2.0。Deprecated and insecure(vulnerable to POODLE attack, deprecated by RFC 6176)
no-ssl3Disabled protocolSSL 3.0。Deprecated and insecure(vulnerable to POODLE attack, deprecated by RFC 7568)
-DOPENSSL_NO_HEARTBEATSCompile macroDisable TLS Heartbeat extension.Heartbleed vulnerabilityroot cause of (CVE-2014-0160)

About no-xxx(disabled algorithms/protocols)

OpenSSL’s ./Configure Accept no-<feature> BeforeRemove at compile timeunnecessary algorithms, effect:

  • Reduce size: disabled algorithms are not compiled into .so, library file is smaller
  • Avoid patents: IDEA, RC5 were patented algorithms (expired, but old versions still need explicit enabling)
  • Eliminate security risks: MD2/SSL2/SSL3 have been proven insecure, no need to keep

Suggestion: If unsure which algorithms are needed,adding all is the safest— can significantly reduce the compiled product size and eliminate known security risk algorithms.

If your project really needs a certain algorithm (e.g., legacy devices that must support SSL3), remove the corresponding no-xxx That’s it.

About -DOPENSSL_NO_HEARTBEATS(compile macro)

OpenSSL 1.0.0 itself does not support Heartbeat(Heartbeat was introduced in 1.0.1), so this macro on 1.0.0 isredundant.
But it’s harmless to include, and if you switch to 1.0.1+ version in the future, it will automatically prevent Heartbleed.

Minimal configuration (do not disable any algorithms)

If you don’t want to disable any algorithms, you can change this line:

simplify to:

Configure will automatically try to compile all algorithms, skipping those that fail to compile. Only when youneed to reduce sizeorexplicitly disable certain algorithmsshould you add no-xxx。

3. Modify Makefile (critical fix)

Configure generated Makefile in,ARFLAGS Default is empty, and AR the definition order is problematic:

The two issues combined cause build failure：

  1. Thin archive issue: For some versions of cross toolchains (including the Arm GNU Toolchain 11.2 used in this article), the ar Before ARFLAGS when empty, by default createsthin archive(thin archive) — the archive only stores the symbol table, does not embed .o file content. When linking, ld reading members will fail:
  1. AR parameter order issue：ar the syntax is ar [-]X[MOPTS] archive [members], the operator (r) must be immediately adjacent to ar or placed before MOPTS the preceding. Wrong order ar rc r will cause ar to treat the last r as the archive filename.

Fix: change ARFLAGS to c(create), and change AR in the definition r and ARFLAGS Swap order.

⚠️ Important: OpenSSL 1.0.0 has 3 Makefile needs to be modified (top-level,crypto/、ssl/), because crypto/Makefile and ssl/Makefile each has its own AR definition, andhardcodedar r(no $(CROSS_COMPILE)prefix). The top-level Makefile passes make -e Pass AR environment variables, but in the sub-Makefile AR the definition has higher priority.

After modification, verify:

Expected output:

Actual call after expansion:aarch64-none-linux-gnu-ar rc ../../libcrypto.a *.o — correct fat archive format.

Principles

  • ar Meaning of the mode letters in the command:

    • r: replace/insert member (operation)

    • c: create archive (if not exists) (modifier)

    • s: write symbol index (equivalent to running separately ranlib）

  • GNU ar syntax:ar [-]X[MOPTS] archive [members]

    • X = operation（r/d/m/p/q/t/x）

    • MOPTS = modifiers（c/v/w/…）

wrong ar $(ARFLAGS) r(becomes when ARFLAGS=c ar c r) will make ar Put r as archive name. The correct way is ar r$(ARFLAGS) → ar rc。

4. Compile (directly make)

After compilation, you will get:

5. Verify the output

6. Installation

Final directory structure:

7. One-click script

Save the following content as build-openssl-aarch64.sh, place it in openssl-1.0.0s/ the same directory:

8. Troubleshooting common issues

SymptomCauseFix
Configure: target already definedCROSS_COMPILE Written as command-line argumentsUse instead export CROSS_COMPILE=... Environment variable
CC= cc(no prefix)Used linux-aarch64 non-existent targets such asUse instead linux-generic64
ld: member ... in archive is not an objectCross ar generates thin archiveModify ARFLAGS/AR in 3 Makefiles (see Section 3)
ar: two different operation options specifiedARFLAGS contains r(with the AR definition’s r duplicate)ARFLAGS should only be c, cannot be rc
ar: creating r or ar: r: No such fileAR parameter order error (ar $(ARFLAGS) r）sed -i 's/AR=...ar $(ARFLAGS) r$/AR=...ar r$(ARFLAGS)/' Makefile
Modified top-level Makefile but archive is still thincrypto/Makefile and ssl/Makefile Has independent AR definitionModify 3 Makefiles at the same time
.so Only a few KBMissing during linking --whole-archive(Very rare, built into Makefile)Check Makefile.shared’s ALLSYMSFLAGS
Compilation reports openssl/idea.h: No such fileNot added no-ideaAdd during Configure no-idea no-mdc2 no-rc5
make clean Reports rm -f install/libinstall/ Directory already exists and is not emptyManually rm -rf install Or ignore this error

9. Summary: Four steps to produce .so
entry.summary: This article describes how to compile OpenSSL 1.0.0s for aarch64 on x86_64 host using ARM cross toolchain to compile OpenSSL 1.0.0s aarch64 shared library complete process, including environment preparation, Configure configuration (need to use linux-aarch64 target and through environment variable CROSS_COMPILE specify toolchain prefix), modify ARFLAGS and AR definitions in three Makefiles to solve thin archive and parameter order issues, as well as compilation, installation and common problem troubleshooting methods.

entry.title: Tag Plugin Plus Plugin Tags Overview
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-11

init

This article provides an overview of Tag Plugin Plus plugin tags, showcasing various plugin tags ported from the Butterfly theme to the Cactus theme, including link cards, inline images, inline text labels, colored paragraphs, prompt boxes, collapsible boxes, timelines, tabs, etc., and provides syntax and effect examples. It also tests the rendering support for standard Markdown and extended syntax.

Markdown Basic Syntax

The following tests the current theme’s rendering support for standard Markdown and extended syntax.

Heading Levels

h1 Level 1 Heading

h2 Level 2 Heading

h3 Level 3 Heading

h4 Level 4 Heading

h5 Level 5 Heading
h6 Level 6 Heading

Inline Formatting

Bold text、Italic text、Bold italic text、Strikethrough text、Inline code

Highlighted text (if supported), superscript X2, subscript H2O (if renderer supports)

Highlight (mark) boundaries

Continuous highlight: first paragraph and second paragraph with normal text in between.

Highlight embedded format: Bold + Code + erase Mixed inline syntax.

Highlighted text within a blockquote: highlighted text.

ScenarioExample
Highlight in tableCell Highlight
Highlight with linkeven629

Links

Normal link

External link

Link with title

even629’s blog

Reference-style link

Hexo Official and Cactus Theme

Automatic link

https://even629.com

Image

List

Unordered list

  • First-level list item A
  • First-level list item B
    • Second-level nested item B1
    • Second-level nested item B2
      • Third-level nested item
  • First-level list item C

Ordered list

  1. Step 1
  2. Step 2
    1. Sub-step A of Step 2
    2. Sub-step B of Step 2
  3. Step 3

Task list

  • Completed task
  • Incomplete task
  • To-do item
    • Nested task item

Task list boundary

  • Read Hexo documentation and Take notes
  • Run hexo s Local preview
  • Submit Draft Official version
  • Normal list item (mixed with task items)
    • Nested task, containing Inline code
    • Nested task completed

Reference

Single-level quote

This is a quote.
It can span multiple lines.

Nested quote

First-level quote

Second-level quote

Third-level quote

Nested elements in a blockquote

Heading inside a blockquote

  • List item 1
  • List item 2

Continue nesting

Code

Inline code

Use in text print() function to output content.

Fenced code block

Code block with specified language

Escaping in code blocks

Table

Basic table

Column AColumn BColumn C
Value 1Value 2Value 3
Value 4Value 5Value 6

Table alignment

Left alignCenter alignRight align
LeftinRight
CellCellCell

Inline table format

nameDescription
BoldBold text
CodeInline code
LinksClick me
MixedBold + Code + erase

Merge cells

Through markdown-it-merge-cells-plus Plugin merges cells on demand: filling in a marker in a cell triggers merging; by default, adjacent identical cells are not merged.

  • < - Merge current cell toLeftCell (horizontal / colspan）
  • ^ - Merge current cell toAboveCell (vertical / rowspan）

Header row can only use < Merge left and right, cannot merge up and down;^ Only effective from the second row of body text (the first row of body text is adjacent to the header and cannot be merged into the header). The merged area must form a rectangle.

Horizontal merge (colspan)

Used for both header and body < Merge left:

CategoryFront-end technology stack
Full stack
BackendNodeGo

Header Front-end technology stack Merge the cell to the right (colspan=2); body Full stack Spans three columns.

Vertical merge (rowspan)

Used for body ^ Merge up:

CategoryProjectDescription
Front-endHTMLMarkup language
CSSStyle language
JSScripting language
BackendNodeRuntime

Front-end Using two ^ Merge down, spanning three rows (rowspan=3）。

Combined merge (colspan + rowspan)

< and ^ Can be mixed in the same table to form merged blocks spanning rows and columns:

RegionQ1
North100
200
South300

Header Region Spans two columns; body North Spans both two rows and two columns (rowspan=2 colspan=2), only the following remains below it 200。

Edge cases (invalid markers displayed as-is)

In the following scenarios, markers do not trigger merging and are rendered literally < / ^ Rendering:

^^ in header displayed as-is
^^ in first row of body displayed as-is
<< in first column displayed as-is
DataNormal cell

In header, ^, in first row of body, ^, in first column, < are all no-ops, displayed as literal text.

Separator line

Footnote

This is a text with a footnote[1], and here is another footnote[2].

Emoji

😆 🚀 ❤️ 👍 💯 🔥 💪 👏

Escape character

*won’t become italic\* `won’t become code\` \~won’t become strikethrough\~

Math formulas (KaTeX)

Inline formula

Mass-energy equation:E=mc2E = mc^2E=mc2

Euler’s formula:eiπ+1=0e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0eiπ+1=0

Block-level formula

∑i=1nxi=x1+x2+⋯+xn\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_ni=1∑n​xi​=x1​+x2​+⋯+xn​

∫abf(x) dx=F(b)−F(a)\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)∫ab​f(x)dx=F(b)−F(a)

[123456789]\begin{bmatrix}1 & 2 & 3 \\4 & 5 & 6 \\7 & 8 & 9\end{bmatrix}​147​258​369​​

Mermaid diagrams

The following tests Mermaid flowchart rendering (this article’s frontmatter has set mermaid: true, enabling the mermaid library).

Flowchart

graph TD A[开始] --> B{是否登录?} B -- 是 --> C[进入主页] B -- 否 --> D[跳转登录] D --> E[输入凭证] E -->|成功| C E -->|失败| D C --> F[结束]

Sequence diagram

sequenceDiagram participant U as 用户 participant S as 服务器 participant D as 数据库 U->>S: 发起请求 S->>D: 查询数据 D-->>S: 返回结果 S-->>U: 响应数据 Note over U,D: 一次完整的请求-响应流程

Gantt chart

gantt title 项目开发计划 dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD section 设计 需求分析 :done, des1, 2026-07-01, 7d 原型设计 :done, des2, after des1, 5d section 开发 前端开发 :active, dev1, after des2, 10d 后端开发 : dev2, after des2, 12d section 测试 集成测试 : tst1, after dev1, 5d 上线部署 : tst2, after tst1, 2d

Pie chart

pie title 博客文章分类占比 "Linux 内核/驱动" : 35 "ARM 架构" : 20 "QEMU 虚拟化" : 15 "编程语言" : 20 "其他" : 10

Class diagram

classDiagram class Animal { +String name +int age +makeSound() void } class Dog { +fetch() void } class Cat { +purr() void } Animal <|-- Dog Animal <|-- Cat

State diagram (stateDiagram-v2)

stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> 草稿 草稿 --> 审核中: 提交 审核中 --> 已发布: 通过 审核中 --> 草稿: 驳回 已发布 --> [*] 草稿 --> [*]: 删除

Styled nodes

graph LR A[普通节点] --> B(圆角节点) B --> C{决策节点} C -->|是| D[/平行四边形/] C -->|否| E[(数据库)] style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#9f9,stroke:#333 style E fill:#69f,stroke:#333,color:#fff

Syntax: use ```mermaid Fenced code blocks. Supports flowchart / sequence / gantt / pie / class / state and other diagram types, see Mermaid documentation. Need to set in frontmatter mermaid: true to load the mermaid library and render.

Tag Plugin Plus external tags

The following shows all external tags ported from Butterfly to Cactus and their variants.

entry.summary: This article provides an overview of Tag Plugin Plus plugin tags, showcasing various plugin tags ported from the Butterfly theme to the Cactus theme, including link cards, inline images, inline text labels, colored paragraphs, prompt boxes, collapsible boxes, timelines, tabs, etc., and provides syntax and effect examples. It also tests the rendering support for standard Markdown and extended syntax.

entry.title: Remoteproc resource table
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-04

init

This article introduces the structure and function of the resource table in the remote processor firmware image, detailing how the main processor, after loading the firmware, decodes the resource table to allocate system resources such as physical memory, configure Virtio devices, and create RPMsg channels for inter-core communication. Additionally, using the STM32MP15C reference code, it summarizes the specific definitions of resource entries under the Remoteproc framework and the implementation of dynamic resource management based on OpenAMP.

Reference code:

STM32CubeMP1 MPU Firmware Package

  • STM32MP157C-EV1 RevC
  • STM32MP157C-DK2 RevC

STM32CubeMP1

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeMP1/

Remote processor firmware image

The firmware image of a remote processor generally includes:

  • Resource table (resource_table)
  • User application
  • RTOS or bare metal (BM) related code
  • OpenAMP library.

After the main processor loads the remote processor’s firmware into the remote processor’s core, it decodes the firmware image to obtain associated resources and reserves memory for the firmware code and data segments. After starting the remote processor, the main processor creates an RPMsg channel, which is used for remote communication.

The resources of the remote processor include:

  • System resources required by the remote processor before power-up, such as contiguous physical memory allocated to the remote processor, and peripherals allocated to the remote processor (these devices can be reserved or unused). These resources are configured in the stm32mp157-m4-srm.dtsi resource manager of the device tree file (i.e., m4_system_resources node).
  • In addition to system resources, the remote processor has a resource table (resource_table), which may also contain resource entries that advertise the functions supported by the remote processor or existing configurations, such as the vring address and vring size in Virtio devices.

Only after meeting all resource requirements (M-core configuration requirements, the resource table will require Linux to allocate corresponding resources, etc.) will Remotecore start the device.

Resource table

Under the kernel source codeinclude/linux/remoteproc.h file, find the following structure resource_table, which is the firmware resource table header:

Linux-defined struct resource_table

Following this firmware resource table header are the resource entries themselves. Each entry starts with a struct fw_rsc_hdr header, and the content of the entry itself follows this header, parsed according to the resource type. Below is the beginning of the resource entry header:

struct fw_rsc_hdr

The resource type is defined as follows:

These values are used as rproc_handle_rsc()function (in remoteproc_internal.h Index of the lookup table in the file).

When registering a new remote processor, the Remoteproc framework will look up its resource table and register the Virtio devices it supports. The firmware should provide information about the Virtio devices it supports and their configuration to Remoteproc,RSC_VDEV The resource entry should specify the Virtio device ID (such as virtio_ids.h), Virtio features, Virtio configuration space, vrings information, etc. We can check the Linux file system/sys/kernel/debug/remoteproc/remoteproc0/resource_table file to get RSC_VDEV information. (That is, throughrproc_handle_rsc()function first look up the relevant attribute, then go toRSC_VDEVto find the specific configuration of that attribute.)

struct fw_rsc_carveout

struct fw_rsc_devmem

struct fw_rsc_trace

struct fw_rsc_vdev_vring

struct fw_rsc_vdev

struct rproc_mem_entry

Implementation of rsc_table for STM32MP15C

Take STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeMP1 the repository’s STM32MP157C-EV1 as an example:

STM32CubeMP1/Projects/STM32MP157C-EV1/Applications/OpenAMP/OpenAMP_Dynamic_ResMgr/Src/rsc_table.c

while STM32CubeMP1/Projects/STM32MP157C-EV1/Applications/OpenAMP/OpenAMP_Dynamic_ResMgr/Inc/rsc_table.h as follows:

attribute tag

  • __resource = __attribute__((section(".resource_table")))

    • Put the entire resource_table variable into .resource_table the ELF section.
    • This is exactly the Linux side rproc_elf_load_rsc_table() / rproc_elf_find_loaded_rsc_table() the section name it looks for — when A7 loads the firmware ELF, it locates the resource table by section name.

  • resource_table_init() Pass the address and length of this table to the OpenAMP middleware, which will be used by the M4’s virtio/rpmsg stack later.

struct shared_resource_table

STM32CubeMP1/Projects/STM32MP157C-EV1/Applications/OpenAMP/OpenAMP_Dynamic_ResMgr/Inc/rsc_table.h

struct fw_rsc_vdev

STM32CubeMP1/Middlewares/Third_Party/OpenAMP/open-amp/lib/include/openamp/remoteproc.h

ST’s fw_rsc_vdev folded type in

There are two points here:

  • ST in struct fw_rsc_vdev added type at the very beginning, while Linux’s struct fw_rsc_vdev has no type (type is in a separate struct fw_rsc_hdr inside). But the two are exactly the same on-wire:

ST does this so that a designated initializer can initialize the entire vdev entry (including type) at once.

  • C trick of zero-length vring[0] + separate vring0/vring1 members

fw_rsc_vring[0] at the end of vdev occupies 0 bytes, so struct shared_resource_table The vring0 and vring1 members immediately following vdev in memory fall right after vdev — that is, where Linux expects to find the vring array. Equivalent to Linux’s flexible array vring[], but ST uses separate named members for ease of initialization.

struct fw_rsc_vdev_vring

STM32CubeMP1/Middlewares/Third_Party/OpenAMP/open-amp/lib/include/openamp/remoteproc.h

struct fw_rsc_trace

STM32CubeMP1/Middlewares/Third_Party/OpenAMP/open-amp/lib/include/openamp/remoteproc.h

rsc_table instance

STM32CubeMP1/Projects/STM32MP157C-EV1/Applications/OpenAMP/OpenAMP_Dynamic_ResMgr/Src/rsc_table.c

vdev

Because this project #define LINUX_RPROC_MASTER（openamp_conf.h), it takes the static initialization branch:

vring

vring fieldsvring0 (TX)vring1 (RX)Description
da-1 (FW_RSC_ADDR_ANY)-1Device address. If -1, it indicates support for dynamically allocated vring device address.
align1616Alignment size. Number of alignment bytes between the vring consumer and producer parts.
num1616Number of buffers. Number of buffers supported by this vring (must be a power of 2).
notifyid0
(master→remote)		1
(remote→master)		Notification ID. Unique notification index within the entire remote processor (rproc). When sending a signal (kick) to the remote, this ID tells it which vring was triggered.
reserved00Reserved field. Must be 0.

Dynamic ResMgr core:da = -1

  • Static method: firmware hardcoded vring.da = 0x10040000, Linux uses directly.
  • Dynamic method (STM32CubeMP1/Projects/STM32MP157C-EV1/Applications/OpenAMP/OpenAMP_Dynamic_ResMgr/): firmware writes da = -1, meaning “Linux allocates”, Linux from DT vdev0vring0/1 reserved carveout allocate vring memory from the pool, fill back the real address to vring->da. Firmware declares requirements, host allocates and fills back — exactly the fw_rsc_vdev comment says “negotiation”.

openamp_conf.h There are two branches:

cm_trace

Note:

  • trace’s da is not -1
  • trace buffer is allocated by M4 itself (system_log_buf in M4 memory), host read-only, not allocate. After host starts, it creates debugfs/.../cm4_log, what it reads is the log written by M4.

resource_table_init()

Preprocessor branch handles three scenarios:

ScenarioConditionBehavior
Linux as master
#ifdef LINUX_RPROC_MASTERSkip the entire #if !defined(LINUX_RPROC_MASTER) block; the table is initialized at compile time, the function only returns:
table_ptr = (void *)&resource_table
length = sizeof(sizeof(resource_table))
GCC + M4 as masterGNUC && !LINUX_RPROC_MASTER
&& VIRTIO_MASTER_ONLY		GCC does not initialize global variables with section attribute at startup, so memset After clearing, fill field by field at runtime
GCC + M4 as slaveGNUC && !LINUX_RPROC_MASTERwhile(vring1.da != VRING_RX_ADDRESS){} Spin and wait for master to fill the table

This set #if The nesting is for OpenAMP to be compatible with Linux-master / M4-master / M4-slave written for three topologies. For STM32MP157 (Linux master), the runtime part is not compiled, it actually only returns a pointer*length = sizeof(resource_table); and *table_ptr = (void *)&resource_table;。

Three #if The branch indicates who fills the table for this M4 code under the three topologies resource_table: Linux loads (static), M4 master fills at runtime, M4 slave waits to fill.

Summary

This rsc_table.c is the “resource requirement list” submitted by M4 firmware to Linux:

  • Declare a virtio-rpmsg device (id=7, 2 vrings, name-service feature)
  • a trace buffer, and set the vring address to -1 (FW_RSC_ADDR_ANY) let Linux dynamically allocate — this is exactly Dynamic ResMgr the meaning.

The entire table is placed in .resource_table ELF section, initialized at compile time,resource_table_init() just pass the pointer to the OpenAMP middleware.

Storage and system resource allocation

reserved-memory

Before stm32mp157d-atk.dtsi This code can be seen under the device tree:

reserved-memory Indicates that the memory allocated under this node is reserved memory,Reserved memory areas are generally used by specific drivers. They are different from the memory areas used by the Linux kernel. Generally, the function of reserved memory is closely related to the Linux kernel’s DMA or CMA.

If a node’s compatible attribute is shared-dma-pool, it means the node’s memory area is used as a shared pool of DMA buffers for a group of devices. At this point, if you see in the node attributes no-map, it means the memory cannot be mapped by the Linux kernel as part of system memory and needs to be separated from system memory. If you see in the node attributes reusable attribute, it means the memory does not need to be separated from system memory. When a specific driver is not using this memory, the OS can use it.

Note that a node cannot have both no-map and reusable attributes because they are logically contradictory.

We see reserved-memory each child node under the node contains no-map attribute, indicating that this memory cannot be used by the Linux kernel as system memory; it is actually reserved for the M4 system.

mcuram2

0x10000000 is the starting address of SRAM1, and the size 0x40000 is exactly 256KB. This area is the SRAM1+SRAM2 region, mainly used to store the code and data segments of the M4 firmware: SRAM1 (code) and SRAM2 (data). This can be seen from the linker script of the M4 project, but the address range can also be redefined by modifying the linker script. However, note that the address range in the linker script must be consistent with the range configured in the device tree.

vdev0vring0、vdev0vring1 、vdev0buffer

vdev0vring0、vdev0vring1 and vdev0buffer The child node is exactly at SRAM3, i.e., the IPC buffer. The allocation of these three nodes is as follows:

  • vdev0vring0 Child node, 0x10040000 is the starting address of vring0, with an address length of 0x1000, i.e., 4KB. Similarly,
  • vdev0vring1 is the starting address of vring1, also with an address length of 0x1000, i.e., 4KB. These two nodes are the vrings we mentioned earlier for sending and receiving messages.
  • vdev0buffer Child node, starting address 0x10042000, address length 0x4000, size 16KB. This address falls within SRAM3, and this is the configured shared memory area.

vdev0vring0、vdev0vring1 and vdev0buffer Only the first 24KB of SRAM3 is occupied. SRAM3 has 64KB and is not fully used, so if needed, the unused addresses of SRAM3 can be used for other functions by modifying the device tree and linker script.

mcuram

mcuram Child node starting address is 0x30000000, address length 0x40000, size 256KB. This address is the SRAM1 and SRAM2 area in RAM aliases. Since the physical addresses of RAM aliases and SRAMs are the same, it is also necessary to configure the corresponding area that is ‘visible’ to A7. This area corresponds to the area ‘visible’ to M4.mcuram2area. Because mcuramand mcuram2 have the same physical address, so the functions of these two storage areas are the same. It can be said that mcuram is an alias storage area for mcuram2. This may be the origin of ‘aliases’ in RAM aliases. Note that in the device tree,mcuram and mcuram2 the memory segment definitions must be consistent.

retram

retram The starting address of the child node is 0x38000000, with an address length of 0x10000 (64KB). It belongs to the RETRAM area in RAM aliases. This area corresponds to the RETRAM area in the BOOT storage area; they are the same physical address. RETRAM is used to store the interrupt vector table of the M4 core (the interrupt vector table starts at 0x00000000). By default, the starting address of RETRAM is 0x38000000, and it is remapped to 0x00000000 to execute M4 code.

Summary

Child nodeAddressSizeRegion
mcuram20x10000000~0x10040000256KBM4 visible SRAM1+SRAM2
vdev0vring00x10040000~0x100410004KBSRAM3
vdev0vring10x10041000~0x100420004KBSRAM3
vdev0buffer0x10042000~0x1004600016KBSRAM3
mcuram0x30000000~0x30040000256KBA7 visible SRAM1+SRAM2
retram0x38000000~0x3801000064KBA7 visible RETRAM

For the convenience of unified address management, the same physical address of a real chip may have different address mappings in A7 and M4. For example, in A7 it starts from 0X38000000 and this part is called mcuram, while in M4 it starts from 0X10000000 and this part is called mcuram2. However, these two addresses and their covered lengths actually point to the same real memory chip.

m4_system_resources

stm32mp151.dtsiDevice tree file

In the above device tree node, there is am4_system_resourceschild node (that is, M4’s resource manager), which is used to configure M4’s peripheral resources, among whichcompatiblein the attribute rproc-srm-core will match the kernel source code’sdrivers/remoteproc/rproc_srm_core.cdriver file, as follows isrproc_srm_core.cpartial code of the file:

After the device and driver match successfully, the function represented by the probe member variable of platform_driver rproc_srm_core_probe() is executed, and through this function, the rproc sub-device is registered (rproc represents a physical remote processor device, which can be said to be a peripheral)

The kernel source code’sstm32mp157-m4-srm.dtsiDevice tree file, as follows,&m4_rproc indicates that in the previousm4_rproc node, append content:

The above codeconfigures the system resources of M4, that is, which peripherals M4 has configured, howeverstatusattributes are alldisabled, that is, although peripherals are configured, they are not enabled.

stm32mp157d-atk.dtsiThe file includesstm32mp157-m4-srm.dtsifile

stm32mp157d-atk.dtsThe file also includes stm32mp157d-atk.dtsi file

So you can directly instm32mp157d-atk.dtsior stm32mp157d-atk.dts In the device tree file, select to enable a certain peripheral of M4.

M4 and A7 share some peripherals, for example, GPIO is a shared resource. If this GPIO is not multiplexed for other functions and is simply used as a normal IO, then both A7 and M4 can access these resources. For example, instm32mp157d-atk.dtsibelow, a buzzer and two LED nodes are configured. They use the GPIO function. These nodes are for A7, but M4 can also use them:

If it is a peripheral with exclusive selection function, that is, these peripherals can only be used by A7 alone or by M4 alone. If both A7 and M4 use the peripheral together, there will be resource contention issues, and one side will experience anomalies (the main processor has certain priority, usually the coprocessor side has anomalies). For example, if A7 and M4 both occupy ADC1 to collect data, then the data collected by M4 will be inaccurate, possibly unable to collect data and showing 0.

For exclusive peripherals:

  • If A7 wants to use this peripheral, the corresponding peripheral node for A7 must be configured in the device tree.
  • If this peripheral is to be used by M4, it is not necessary to configure the M4-related nodes in the device tree. It is only necessary to configure the peripheral in the firmware (i.e., configure it in the bare-metal program). After A7 loads and starts the firmware, M4 can use the peripheral.

If a peripheral node corresponding to A7 has already been configured in the device tree, and M4 wants to use this peripheral, it is best to comment out the relevant nodes occupied by A7 in the device tree. For example, instm32mp157d-atk.dtsithe device tree has the following nodes:

The above code segment indicates that ADC1 is allocated to A7. If M4 wants to use it, this code does not need to be commented out, as long as it is ensured that after the Linux system runs, A7 does not operate ADC1. Then, after loading and running the M4 firmware (the firmware has already configured ADC1), M4 can use ADC1 to collect data.

However, if A7 operates ADC1 at this time, the data collected by ADC1 on the M4 side will be inaccurate. Therefore, it is recommended to comment out:

That is, comment out the ADC1 part occupied by A7. The following &m4_adc node part is manually added. This part can be added or not, but according to ST’s standard, it is best to add it. The device tree stm32mp157c-dk2-m4-examples.dts is a template file. When modifying the device tree, you can refer to the template file. The above modified&m4_adcnode is written by referring to this file.

After modifying the device tree, execute the following command to recompile the device tree:

Then, the compiledstm32mp157d-atk.dtbfile is copied to the development board’s file system/bootdirectory, replace the previous device tree binary file, then execute the sync command to synchronize the cache, then restart the development board. After re-entering the Linux operating system, A7 can no longer operate ADC1. When the M4 firmware is loaded and started, M4 can access ADC1 alone.

Linker script

Linker script syntax

Entry address

ENTRY(SYMBOL), indicates setting the value of the symbol SYMBOL to the entry address, i.e., the address of the first instruction executed by the program.

Memory region definition

By default, the linker can allocate storage areas at arbitrary positions for sections. The MEMORY command can be used to describe which memory regions can be used by the linker and which should be avoided. A linker script can contain at most one MEMORY command.

  • NAME is the name used to reference the memory region in the linker script, each memory region has a unique name

  • ORIGINis the start address

  • LENGTH is the length of the address

  • ATTR: optional attribute string used to restrict which input sections can be placed in this region. Supported characters include:

    • r: Read-only section
    • w: Read/Write section
    • x: Executable section
    • a: Allocatable section
    • i or l: Initialized section
    • !: Inverted attribute (indicates placing sections that do not satisfy any attribute after this character)

Usage: Once a memory region is defined, it can be used in section descriptions >region to instruct the linker to place a specific output section into that region.

Section linking definition

The SECTIONS command is a very important command in the linker script. Its function is:to tell the linker how to map input file sections to output file sections, and how to place output sections into the address space。

Like the MEMORY command, a linker script has only one SECTIONS command. If there is no SECTIONS command in the entire linker script, the linker will combine all input sections with the same name into one output section, and the order of input sections is the order in which they are discovered by the linker.

Some common usages:

  • . = ALIGN(4): indicates 4-byte address alignment. That is, the start address of the section must be divisible by 4. Common ones are ALIGN(4) or ALIGN(8), i.e., 4-byte or 8-byte alignment.

  • PROVIDE and PROVIDE_HIDDEN Keyword: indicates defining a symbol in the linker script file that is not defined by the object file but is referenced by the object file.

  • KEEP(): The role of KEEP() is that when the linker’s–gc-sections garbage collection option is enabled, this part cannot be reclaimed. For example, KEEP(*(.text))Indicates that not all .text sections can be treated as garbage.

  • /DISCARD/: is a special section name. If this section name is used as output, then all matching sections are discarded.

For more linker script syntax, refer to this site:

GNU LD

https://even629.com/posts/50001/

stm32mp15xx_m4.ld

Analysis:

  1. Core configuration analysis

    • Program entry: Specify Reset_Handler as the program entry. This function is in the startup file startup_stm32mp15xx.s defined in.

    • Stack top address: Set the highest stack address to 0x10040000. It determines the initial position of the stack pointer (SP), corresponding to the mapping boundary of the internal SRAM.

    • Stack size: Specify minimum space. Heap size is 512B, Stack size is 1KB。

  2. Memory region (MEMORY) definition

    • m_interrupts (0x00000000 ~ 0x00000298): Mapped to RETRAM area, used to store the interrupt vector table of the M4 core.

    • m_text (0x10000000 ~ 0x10020000): Corresponds to SRAM1 (128KB), used to store code section (Code).

    • m_data (0x10020000 ~ 0x10040000): Corresponds to SRAM2 (128KB), used to store data section (Data).

The linker script plans the program execution address in SRAM:

  1. SRAM allocation logic under dual-core coordination

Total SRAM space available for M4 is SRAM1 ~ SRAM4 (total 384KB), address range 0x10000000 ~ 0x1005FFFF. When allocating, pay attention to the operating mode:

  • Single-core mode (only M4 running): SRAM1 ~ SRAM4 can be fully allocated to M4.
  • Dual-core mode (A7 + M4 running)：
    • SRAM1 & SRAM2: Fully allocated to M4 (stores M4 code and data).
    • SRAM3: Shared by A7 and M4. Among them, 0x10040000 ~ 0x10046000 by default asmemory exchange area for dual-core communication (IPC Shared Memory). The specific allocation ratio needs to refer to the Device Tree configuration under Linux.

And m_interrupts is actually in RETRAM, what is the remaining SRAM4 used for?

  • If not running Linux OS, but only running M4 bare-metal program, the M4 core can fully use this area, as specified by the user.

  • If running Linux OS, under the Linux device tree, SRAM4 is already defaulted as DMA for Linux functions. To release this area, delete the corresponding node in the device tree (but not recommended, A7 may become abnormal).

Based on the above analysis, the summary is as follows:

  • If not running A7, only running M4 (M4 can run bare-metal, RTOS): SRAM1~SRAM4 can be fully allocated to M4;
  • If running both A7 and M4 (e.g., dual-core communication): SRAM1 and SRAM2 are exclusively for M4, part of SRAM3 is used by both M4 and A7, SRAM4 is configured as DMA under Linux, i.e., occupied by A7. If you need to modify the address range in MEMORY, you must refer to the address range of the memory map table to modify.

References

[1]
Remoteproc Brief analysis of related drivers ---- Resource table
[2]
Remoteproc Brief analysis of related drivers ---- Storage and system resource allocation
[3]
Remoteproc Brief analysis of related drivers ---- linker script
[4]
ALIENTEKSTM32MP157core board
entry.summary: This article introduces the structure and function of the resource table in the remote processor firmware image, detailing how the main processor, after loading the firmware, decodes the resource table to allocate system resources such as physical memory, configure Virtio devices, and create RPMsg channels for inter-core communication. Additionally, using the STM32MP15C reference code, it summarizes the specific definitions of resource entries under the Remoteproc framework and the implementation of dynamic resource management based on OpenAMP.

entry.title: OpenAMP
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-04

init

This article introduces the background of the OpenAMP open asymmetric multiprocessing software framework and its application in multi-core heterogeneous systems. It discusses in detail the three core components: Virtio, RPMsg, and Remoteproc, and summarizes the specific working mechanism of lifecycle management and message communication between the Linux main processor and bare-metal or RTOS coprocessors based on shared memory and inter-core interrupts.

OpenAMP (Open Asymmetric Multiprocessing) was originally developed by Mentor Graphics and Xilinx to solve the communication problem between RTOS or bare-metal programs and Linux interfaces in AMP systems. Currently, OpenAMP can be used on platforms such as ST, NXP, TI, and Xilinx. These manufacturers have provided ported OpenAMP development examples, and users can refer to these examples for development. This article takes STM32Cube_FW_MP1_V1.2.0\Projects\STM32MP157C-DK2\Applications\OpenAMP as a reference example.

OpenAMP Project Official Website

OpenAMP Project

https://www.openampproject.org/

OpenAMP Official Documentation

OpenAMP Project Documentation

https://openamp.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html

OpenAMP Github Open Source Repository

OpenAMP

https://github.com/OpenAMP

The main OpenAMP library (implementing RPMSG, Virtio, and Remoteproc for RTOS etc)

open-amp

https://github.com/OpenAMP/open-amp

STM32CubeMP1 MPU Firmware Package

  • STM32MP157C-EV1 RevC
  • STM32MP157C-DK2 RevC

STM32CubeMP1

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeMP1/

OpenAMP provides implementations of RPMsg, VirtIO, and RemoteProc:

virtio

Virtio is a virtual device framework that provides shared memory management. The vring in Virtio is a FIFO queue of pointers to data buffers. There are two unidirectional vrings:

  • One vring is dedicated to messages sent to the remote processor
  • The other vring is for messages received from the remote processor

The two vrings form a ring. Data between A7 and M4 is shared through the vring buffers. The vring buffers are the shared memory of the two processors (also called IPC Buffers or Vring buffers).

In the reference configuration provided by ST, the shared memory of STM32MP157 is in SRAM3. As shown in the figure below, the processors complete data forwarding through the vring ring buffer.

The relevant code is located atlinux-5.10.256/drivers/virtio

rpmsg

The RPMsg framework is shown in the figure below. It can be seen that the RPMsg framework is on top of Virtio. The RPMsg (Remote Processor Messaging) framework is a message bus based on Virtio. RPMsg can communicate with the remote side via Virtio under the coordination of IPCC, while Remoteproc can write firmware to the remote side under the control of IPCC, or control the lifecycle and status of the remote side.

It can be seen from the figure that OpenAMP uses IPCC at the bottom layer. Reference:

IPCC

https://even629.com/posts/202607020/

On Cortex-A7, the RemoteProc framework activates Linux-based inter-process communication (IPC) based on the available information in the coprocessor’s firmware resource table. The RPMsg service is implemented through the RPMsg framework, and the mailbox service is implemented by the mailbox driver stm32_ipcc.

On Cortex-M4, the RPMsg service is implemented by the OpenAMP library, and the mailbox service is implemented by the HAL_IPCC driver.

This is a bit convoluted. The general idea is that the A core running Linux and the M core running bare-metal or FreeRTOS communicate through OpenAMP. The A core and M core have correspondingly designed OpenAMP, but they are compatible, only the underlying calls are different. For example, the A core mailbox driver is stm32_ipcc implementation, the M core mailbox service is by HAL_IPCC driver implementation.

In the default configuration provided by ST, the SRAM3 area in MCUSRAM has two vrings, one for sending and one for receiving messages. The vring buffers are the shared memory. The RPMsg framework is based on Virtio’s vrings. It sends messages to the remote processor or receives messages from the remote processor through Virtio’s vrings. When a new message is already in the shared memory, the mailbox framework notifies the processor that there is a message to receive.

RPMsg is actually a message bus based on Virtio, used to implement message passing (transferring inter-core data). RPMsg can be considered a channel for communicating with a remote processor, also called an RPMsg device. Each channel has a local source address and a remote destination address. Messages are transmitted between the source and destination addresses. The communication process is shown in the figure below:

For RPMsg, refer to:

Rpmsg Char Driver

https://even629.com/posts/202606290/

Rpmsg Core

https://even629.com/posts/202606270/

Virtio Rpmsg Bus

https://even629.com/posts/202606230/

Based on these software frameworks, all data is transmitted over RPMsg. RPMsg transfers data to the RPMsg client in the kernel layer, and then to user space through device nodes under the kernel. Combined with the hierarchical relationship of the network TCP/IP structure, shared memory and inter-core interrupts correspond to the physical hardware layer, Virtio corresponds to the MAC sublayer, and RPMsg corresponds to the transport layer, as shown in the following figure:

remoteproc

For SOCs with asymmetric multiprocessing, different cores may run different operating systems. For example, the Cortex-A7 of the STM32MP157 runs the Linux operating system, while the Cortex-M4 can run the OneOS operating system or bare-metal programs.

To facilitate easy communication between the main processor running Linux and the coprocessor, the Remoteproc inter-core communication framework was introduced after Linux version 3.4.x. The Remoteproc framework was developed by Texas Instruments, and on this basis, Mentor Graphics developed a software framework called OpenAMP. Under this framework, the Linux operating system on the main processor can manage the lifecycle of the remote processor and its related software environment, i.e., start or stop the remote processor.

Let’s take the STM32MP157 as an example to look at the Remoteproc framework for M4 and A7, as shown in the following figure:

Remoteproc is a generic remote processing framework, and its functions are:

  1. A7 loads the code and data segments of the M4 firmware image into M4 memory for in-place program execution;
  2. Parse the firmware resource table to set associated resources (information such as the start address and size of each segment in the firmware, Virtio device features, vring address, size, and alignment information);
  3. Control the start and stop of the M4 core firmware;
  4. Establish an RPMsg communication channel for communication with M4;
  5. Provide monitoring and debugging remote services (using sysfs and debugfs file systems, which are already configured by default in the development board’s Linux file system and can be used immediately upon boot).

Note: RPMsg communication uses Mailbox, but this is not well reflected in the diagram. The general process can be understood as: Remoteproc loads the firmware into the M core, and message passing needs to go through the channel established after initialization. In the diagram, RCC etc. are configured by stm32 after the firmware image is loaded._rproc automatically configures, i.e., stm32_rproc is the backend driver of Remoteproc and performs a series of subsequent operations

stm32_rproc is the driver for the remote processor (M4 core), and its functions are:

  1. Register vendor-specific functions (such as callback functions) with the Remoteproc framework;
  2. Handle platform resources associated with M4 (e.g., registers, watchdog, reset, clock, and memory);
  3. Forward notifications to M4 through the mailbox framework.

The firmware mentioned above is the executable file for M4, such as those compiled under MDK .axf files or those compiled under STM32CubeIDE.elf files.

A7 is called the main processor, M4 is called the coprocessor or remote processor. The main processor starts first, then boots the coprocessor:

  • The main processor can first load the firmware of the coprocessor through the Remoteproc framework, then parse the information published in the firmware resource table to configure the coprocessor system resources and create Virtio devices.
  • Once the Remoteproc on the main processor loads and starts the firmware of the remote processor, the coprocessor begins to run.
  • After the coprocessor starts, inter-core communication between the two cores is not yet possible. It is necessary to first create an RPMsg channel, and after the main processor sends the first message, inter-core communication can proceed, allowing data exchange.
  • If it is necessary to stop the firmware, the main processor can also shut down the firmware, and the coprocessor program will stop running accordingly. In summary, the Remoteproc framework implements lifecycle management (LCM) and control of the remote coprocessor, as shown in the following figure:

The relevant code is located atlinux-5.10.256/drivers/remoteproc

Summary

Based on the above analysis, the OpenAMP open-source software framework has the following functions and features:

  • Provides remote processor lifecycle management and inter-processor communication functions;
  • Provides independent libraries that can be used in RTOS and bare-metal software environments;
  • Compatible with upstream Linux Remoteproc, RPMsg, and VirtIO components.

The process of using OpenAMP to achieve inter-core communication:

Assume that the main processor is already running and the remote processor is in a certain state, such as standby or power-off state.

  1. Based on the Remoteproc framework, the main processor first loads the firmware of the remote processor into memory.
  2. Then the main processor starts the remote processor (runs the firmware) and waits for it to complete initialization, such as waking up, de-asserting reset, powering on, etc.
  3. After the remote processor completes initialization, it notifies the main processor.
  4. The main processor and the remote processor establish an RPMsg communication channel.
  5. Through the RPMsg channel, the two can perform inter-core communication.

References

[1]
【STM32MP157 Heterogeneous Core Communication Framework Study】（3）OpenAMPFramework
entry.summary: This article introduces the background of the OpenAMP open asymmetric multiprocessing software framework and its application in multi-core heterogeneous systems. It discusses in detail the three core components: Virtio, RPMsg, and Remoteproc, and summarizes the specific working mechanism of lifecycle management and message communication between the Linux main processor and bare-metal or RTOS coprocessors based on shared memory and inter-core interrupts.

entry.title: IPCC
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-07-02

init

This article introduces the working principle of the IPCC (Inter-Process Communication Controller) hardware module in the STM32MP157, discusses its process of implementing signal notification between the Cortex-A7 and Cortex-M4 cores through shared memory and hardware interrupt mechanisms, and summarizes the channel allocation, software framework application, and related register configuration of the IPCC.

IPC（ Inter -Process Communication, inter-process communication) refers to the interaction between data of two processes (broadly inter-process, including processes of heterogeneous cores), which is achieved through the inter-processor communication controller IPCC（Inter-Process Communication controller , IPC controller) to implement. The IPCC is a hardware part used for signal exchange between two CPUs. The mailbox relies on the IPCC. The IPCC hardware module of the STM32MP157 has 6 bidirectional channels, each channel is divided into two sub-channels, so 6 bidirectional channels have a total of 12 sub-channels. The figure shows a structural block diagram of communication between the A7 core and the M4 core through the IPCC.

When a data packet is placed into the shared memory, the CPU needs to interrupt or ‘notify’ the other CPU that there is a new data packet in the shared memory to be processed. The other CPU processes it after receiving the signal. This is done using the IPCC hardware interrupt mechanism.。

The interrupt controller of the A7 core is GIC, and the interrupt controller of the M4 core is NVIC. The role of the IPCC is like a sentinel, controlling when data can be received and when data can be sent. The entire process is controlled by the IPCC.

The IPCC only provides a mechanism for information exchange between processors; it does not have data transmission capability., that is,The data to be exchanged between processorsis not transmitted in the IPCC,but is transmitted in the shared memory.。

IPCC channels

The working modes of the 6 communications of the IPCC can be divided into simplex, half-duplex, and full-duplex:

  • There are 6 channels in the direction from CPU1 to CPU2 (P1_TO_P2 sub-channel, P1 represents CPU1, P2 represents CPU2)
  • There are 6 channels in the direction from CPU2 to CPU1 (P2_TO_P1 sub-channel, P1 represents CPU1, P2 represents CPU2)

As shown in the table below, in the ST official configuration of the IPCC communication model, these 6 channels are used as different software frameworks, among which:

  • Channel 3 is in simplex mode and is used as the RemoteProc framework. The main processor can load the firmware of the coprocessor and control the lifecycle of the coprocessor through this framework;
  • Channel 2 is in full-duplex mode and is used as the RPMsg framework for transmitting messages from A7 to M4;
  • Channel 1 is in full-duplex mode and is used as the RPMsg framework for transmitting messages from M4 to A7.
ChannelModeUsageSoftware client framework: Cortex-A7 (non-secure)Software Client Framework: Cortex-M4
Channel 1Full DuplexRPMsg Transmission from Cortex-M4 to Cortex-A7:
1. Cortex-M4 uses this channel to indicate that a message is available
2. Cortex-A7 uses this channel to indicate that the message has been processed		RPMsg FrameworkOpenAMP
Channel 2Full DuplexRPMsg Transmission from Cortex-A7 to Cortex-M4:
1. Cortex-A7 uses this channel to indicate that a message is available
2. Cortex-M4 uses this channel to indicate that the message has been processed		RPMsg FrameworkOpenAMP
Channel 3SimplexCortex-M4 Shutdown RequestRemoteProc FrameworkCprocSync cube utility
Channels 4/5/6Unused

Simplex Communication: Refers to signals transmitted in only one direction, can only send or receive

Half-Duplex Communication: Signals can be transmitted in both directions, but only sending or receiving is allowed at a given time (single bus and I2C are both half-duplex communication)

Full-Duplex Communication: Full-duplex communication means data is transmitted in both directions simultaneously

It can be seen that in the framework configured by ST:

  1. The RemoteProc software framework solves the problem of remote processor lifecycle
  2. The RPMsg software framework solves the problem of inter-core communication

These two are the concerns in multi-core processor communication!

Under the Linux kernel, ST has already ported the RemoteProc and RPMsg software frameworks. In the OpenAMP library used by M4, there are also corresponding RemoteProc and RPMsg software frameworks. M4 mainly relies on the existing software framework in the OpenAMP library to communicate with A7.

Note: In order for the main core to control the coprocessor, the OpenAMP framework was designed on the Linux side. However, to be compatible with the Linux-side OpenAMP framework, the relevant M4 vendors also designed an OpenAMP framework. The two can be connected, but there are still some differences, although the name is the same.

The A7 and M4 of STM32MP157 actually transmit data through channel 1 and channel 2:

IPCC registers

There are 8 registers related to IPCC. The register area is divided into two regions, each occupied by one processor to prevent read-write access conflicts. As shown in the table below, the first 4 registers are occupied by processor 1, and the last 4 registers are occupied by processor 2.

IPCC provides dedicated interrupts for processors. Each processor has its own status mask register bits and set or clear register bits for each channel.

RegisterDescriptionBit (n represents 1~6)
IPCC_C1CREnable/Disable Processor 1 TX channel idle interrupt/RX channel occupied interruptTXFIE、RXOIE
IPCC_C1MRMask/Unmask Processor 1 TX channel X idle interrupt/RX channel X occupied interruptCHnFM、CHnOM
IPCC_C1SCRProcessor 1 TX/RX channel X status bit set/clearCHnS、CHnC
IPCC_C1TOC2SRChannel occupied/idleCHnF
IPCC_C2CREnable/Disable Processor 2 TX channel idle interrupt/RX channel occupied interruptTXFIE、RXOIE
IPCC_C2MRMask/Unmask Processor 2 TX channel X idle interrupt/RX channel X occupied interruptCHnFM、CHnOM
IPCC_C2SCRProcessor 2 TX/RX channel X status bit set/clearCHnS、CHnC
IPCC_C2TOC1SRChannel occupied/idleCHnF

IPCC_C1CR

IPCC Processor 1 Control Register

31302928272625242322212019181716
ResResResResResResResResResResResResResResResTXFIE
rw
1514131211109876543210
ResResResResResResResResResResResResResResResRXOIE
rw
  • Bits 17~31 and bits 1~15 are reserved bits and must be kept at reset value.
  • Bit 16, TXFIE: Processor 1 transmit (TX) channel idle interrupt enable bit, with IPCC_C1TOC2SRassociated, when this bit is:
    • 1: Enable processor 1 transmit (TX) channel idle interrupt;
    • 0: Disable processor 1 transmit (TX) channel idle interrupt.
  • Bit 0, RXOIE: Processor 1 receive (RX) channel occupancy interrupt enable bit, associated withIPCC_C2TOC1SRassociated, when this bit is:
    • 1: Enable processor 1 receive (RX) channel occupancy interrupt;
    • 0: Disable processor 1 receive (RX) channel occupancy interrupt.

IPCC_C1MR

IPCC Processor 1 Mask Register

31302928272625242322212019181716
ResResResResResResResResResResCH6FMCH5FMCH4FMCH3FMCH2FMCH1FM
rwrwrwrwrwrw
1514131211109876543210
ResResResResResResResResResResCH6OMCH5OMCH4OMCH3OMCH2OMCH1OM
rwrwrwrwrwrw

  • Bits 22-31 and bits 6-15, reserved bits, must be kept at reset value.

  • Bits 16-21,CH1FM~CH6FM, processor 1 transmit (TX) channel X idle interrupt mask bit, associated with IPCC_C1TOC2SR.CHxFassociated, when this bit is:

    • 1: Transmit (TX) channel X idle interrupt is masked;
    • 0: Transmit (TX) channel X idle interrupt is not masked.

  • Bits 0 and 5,CH1OM CH6OM, processor 1 receive (RX) channel X occupancy interrupt mask bit, associated withIPCC_C2TOC1SR.CHxFrelated, when this bit is:

    • 1: Receive (RX) channel X occupancy interrupt is masked;
    • 0: Receive (RX) channel X occupancy interrupt is not masked.

IPCC_C1SCR

IPCC Processor 1 Status Set Clear Register

31302928272625242322212019181716
ResResResResResResResResResResCH6FMCH5FMCH4FMCH3FMCH2FMCH1FM
rwrwrwrwrwrw
1514131211109876543210
ResResResResResResResResResResCH6OMCH5OMCH4OMCH3OMCH2OMCH1OM
rwrwrwrwrwrw
  • Bits 22-31 and bits 6-15, reserved bits, must be kept at reset value.
  • Bits 16-21, CH1S~CH6S, processor 1 transmit (TX) channel X status set bit, associated withIPCC_C1TOC2SR.CHxF related, when setting this bit:
    • 1: Processor 1 transmit (TX) channel X status bit is set, i.e., CHnF is set to 1, indicating the channel is occupied;
    • 0: No action.
  • Bits 0-5, CH1C~CH6C, processor 1 receive (RX) channel X status clear bit, associated withIPCC_C2TOC1SR.CHxFCorrelation, when this bit is set:
    • 1: Processor 1 receive (RX) channel X status bit cleared;
    • 0: No action

IPCC Functional Description

IPCC controls processor communication through interrupts, called inter-processor interrupts (IPI). Each processor has two interrupt lines:

  • One for RX channel occupancy (transmitting processor publishes data);
  • One for TX channel free (receiving processor reads communication data).

Each channel has interrupt masking:

  • Channel occupancy mask (bit CHnOM);
  • Channel free mask (bit CHnFM).

In the context of computers, microcontrollers, or hardware peripherals (such as DMA, Mailbox, IPCC and other communication controllers),“Mask” means to disable or filter out an interrupt signal, preventing it from being reported to the CPU.。

Each sub-channel has two operating modes:

  • Simplex mode (each channel has its own communication data storage location);
  • Half-duplex mode (a single channel associated with a bidirectional communication data storage location).

The communication data is located in shared memory (note: shared memory is not part of the IPCC). Each communication, the IPCC module provides achannel status flag bit CHnF(n can be 1~6, indicating the corresponding channel):

  • When CHnF=0, it indicates that the associated channel is free(it can also be considered that the communication data has been read by the receiving processor, and the channel is now in an idle state),At this time, the transmitting processor can occupy this channel to send data;
  • When CHnF=1, it indicates that the associated channel is already occupied(i.e., the communication data has been published by the transmitting processor),At this time, the receiving processor can access the channel to read data.

IPCC provides a non-blocking signaling mechanism for channel management:

  • Message availability interrupt;
  • Channel flow control (for example, generating a TX channel idle interrupt is called flow on, and when the channel is occupied, it is called flow off).

Applications benefit from non-blocking interrupt-based message exchange and channel flow control for inter-core communication, with each sub-channel having one transmission direction:

  • Sent from CPU1 and received by CPU2;
  • Or sent from CPU2 and received by CPU1.

Simplex transmission mode

The following figure shows a schematic diagram of the IPCC simplex transmission mode.

  • When the channel status flag indicates channel occupied (CHnF=1), this is because the receiver has not released the channel from the previous message. At this time, the channel idle interrupt is unmasked, and then it waits for the TX idle interrupt.
  • Once the receiver releases the channel, a channel idle interrupt (flow on) is generated. When the channel idle interrupt is generated, the channel idle interrupt is masked, and the message can be written to the data buffer. Subsequently, the channel status flag is set to occupied, which triggers the channel occupied interrupt at the receiver.

Simplex reception mode

The following figure shows a schematic diagram of the IPCC simplex transmission mode. When a channel occupied interrupt is generated, the receiver determines which channel is occupied and masks the corresponding channel occupied interrupt. Subsequently, data can be read from the shared memory. Once data is read, the channel status flag CHnF is cleared (CHnF=0), and the channel occupied interrupt is unmasked.

Half-duplex transmission mode

The following figure shows a schematic diagram of the IPCC half-duplex transmission mode.

The half-duplex process allows using a single shared buffer to transmit a message from the sender to the receiver, and then send a response (response pending) from the receiver back to the sender.

Sender data transmission process:

  • The software variable response pending=1 indicates that the sender is waiting for a response from the receiver.
  • response pending=0 indicates that the response from the receiver has been obtained.

First, the sender checks the channel status flag. If the channel status flag indicates the channel is occupied (CHnF=1), meaning the receiver has not yet sent a response to the previous message, the sender waits for the response. If the channel is idle, the sender can write the message to the shared memory.

Subsequently, the channel status flag is set to occupied, which triggers the RX channel occupied interrupt at the receiver. Once the channel status flag CHnF=1, the channel idle interrupt is unmasked. The channel idle interrupt indicates whether the response sent by the receiver is available. When a channel idle interrupt is generated (response ready), the sender determines which channel to release and masks the corresponding channel idle interrupt. Then, the response can be read from the shared memory.

Receiver response process:

The receiving processor waits for the software variable response pending to be equal to 1. If it is equal to 1, the receiver writes the response to the shared memory (publishes the response). Once the response is published, the channel status flag CHnF is cleared by CHnC, so CHnF becomes 0, and the receiver unmasks the channel occupied interrupt (CHnOM = 0).

Half-duplex reception mode

The diagram below shows the IPCC half-duplex reception mode schematic.

  • Receiver reads data: When a channel occupancy interrupt occurs, the receiver determines which channel is occupied, masks the corresponding channel occupancy interrupt, then receives data from the shared memory. The channel is released only after the receiver sends a response to the shared memory.
  • Sender reads response: To receive the response, the channel idle interrupt is unmasked. The sending processor checks which channel becomes idle, masks the related channel idle interrupt, and then reads the response from the shared memory.

IPCC provides a non-blocking signaling mechanism for 6 bidirectional channels to atomically publish and retrieve communication data. The non-blocking method allows processors to exchange information in a non-blocking manner, such as reading data. When conditions are not met and results cannot be obtained immediately, the process is suspended, and then periodically checks whether the conditions are met. If met, the read operation is performed; if not, no read is performed. This is the non-blocking method.

The communication process described above can be represented by the following diagram. All communicating processors can access the shared memory. Around the shared memory, an interrupt line can be configured from the main processor to the coprocessor and from the coprocessor to the main processor, namely**Inter-processor interrupt (PPI, also called core interrupt)**The initiator of the core interrupt first writes the message data to the shared memory, then initiates the inter-core interrupt. The interrupted core thread reads the shared memory in the interrupt service routine to obtain the data notified by the initiator.

Mailbox framework

The Mailbox framework is closely related to IPCC. Mailbox is a driver architecture that relies on the hardware platform for implementation. For example, the Mailbox on the STM32MP157 platform depends on the IPCC peripheral. Its Mailbox framework is shown in the figure below: (Mailbox is a tool for message transmission, relying on IPCC at the bottom layer, but IPCC does not transmit data. Mailbox can transfer message data based on IPCC.)

How does the upper-layer application know that data has been sent or received completely? This task is handled by the Mailbox framework. The implementation of Mailbox is divided into the Mailbox controller and the Mailbox client:

  • The Mailbox controller is mainly responsible for configuring and processing message queues or interrupt requests (IRQ) from the IPCC peripheral, and provides a common API for the Mailbox client responsible for sending or receiving notification messages.

  • The Mailbox client is mainly responsible for sending or receiving notification messages. It sends or receives notification messages through the channel provided by the Mailbox controller, which is the IPCC channel.

Users can define their own Mailbox client. As shown in the figure above, the Mailbox controller under Linux is stm32_ipcc, which configures and controls the IPCC peripheral and can provide mailbox services.stm32_rproc It is the remote processor platform driver, which mainly handles platform resources associated with the remote processor (such as registers, watchdog, reset, clock, and memory). It can register corresponding callback functions into the Remoteproc framework, and can also forward notification messages to the remote processor through the mailbox framework.

The general workflow of the mailbox framework is:

  1. First, register the mailbox controller;
  2. Before sending data, the mailbox client applies for a channel;
  3. The client sends data;
  4. The mailbox client records data;
  5. The mailbox controller calls back the data received from the lower layer to the upper-layer application;
  6. When data transmission is complete, the mailbox controller notifies the upper layer that the current data has been sent;
  7. The email client releases the channel.

Regarding the Mailbox framework, in the Linux kernel source code drivers/mailbox There are related drivers in the directory.

References

[1]
【STM32MP157 Heterogeneous Core Communication Framework Study】（2）IPCCCommunication Framework
entry.summary: This article introduces the working principle of the IPCC (Inter-Process Communication Controller) hardware module in the STM32MP157, discusses its process of implementing signal notification between the Cortex-A7 and Cortex-M4 cores through shared memory and hardware interrupt mechanisms, and summarizes the channel allocation, software framework application, and related register configuration of the IPCC.

entry.title: Rpmsg Char Driver
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-06-29

init

This article introduces the implementation mechanism of the Rpmsg Char Driver character device driver based on Linux 5.10.x. It discusses in detail the classic architecture design where the control device and endpoint device are separated, and summarizes the driver registration, delayed endpoint creation, lock and wait queue synchronization mechanisms, as well as the specific flow and key code logic for user-space message sending and receiving.

The current code analysis is based on Linux 5.10.x

rpmsg_char.c Implements rpmsg_driver and registers on the Rpmsg Bus, providing an interface for user-space interaction, undertaking virtio_rpmsg_bus.c registered rpmsg_device

module_init / module_exit

rpmsg_char.c is a character device driver, with the following hierarchy:

Note that it uses postcore_initcall, earlier than module_init earlier, because rpmsg_core.c the bus also uses postcore_initcall, both are registered after the driver model is ready.

rpmsg_char_init does three things:

  1. alloc_chrdev_region(&rpmsg_major, 0, RPMSG_DEV_MAX, "rpmsg") —— Dynamically allocate a major device number,RPMSG_DEV_MAX = MINORMASK+1(i.e., the entire minor device number space).
  2. class_create(THIS_MODULE, "rpmsg")—— Create /sys/class/rpmsg, so that cdev_device_add afterwards automatically in /dev/ generate device nodes under (depends on udev/devtmpfs）。
  3. register_rpmsg_driver(&rpmsg_chrdev_driver) —— Register an rpmsg driver (not a platform driver! It is a driver attached to the rpmsg bus).

while rpmsg_chrdev_driver as follows:

Key Data Structures

typeDevice NodestructWho createsFunction
Control Device/dev/rpmsg_ctrl0rpmsg_ctrldevrpmsg_chrdev_probeOne per channel; users “create endpoints” on it via ioctl
Endpoint Device/dev/rpmsg0rpmsg_eptdevrpmsg_ctrldev_ioctl(CREATE)One endpoint per user; user reads/writes/polls it to send and receive messages

This separation of ‘control device + data device’ is a classic pattern in Linux:The control node is used to instantiate the data node, and the data node carries the actual IO。

struct rpmsg_ctrldev

struct rpmsg_eptdev

Lock and wait queue:

  • struct mutex ept_lock;: Protect ept pointer. ept becomes NULL in three cases:release、destroy ioctl, parent device remove. The read/write path must first acquire this lock to confirm ept is still alive.
  • spinlock_t queue_lock;: Protect queue (skb linked list). Callback rpmsg_ept_cbEnqueues in interrupt/atomic context, read_iter dequeues in process context, so a spinlock is used here and in cb use spin_lock, in read use spin_lock_irqsave。
  • wait_queue_head_t readq;：rpmsg_ept_cb After enqueuing wake_up_interruptible，read_iter When the queue is empty wait_event_interruptible. This is a typical ‘producer-consumer + wait queue’.

rpmsg_driver

Note: No callback function is defined here

rpmsg_chrdev_probe()

When the rpmsg bus assigns a rpmsg_device match to rpmsg_chrdev_driver called whenrpmsg_chrdev_probe. Process:

  1. kzalloc a ctrldev,ctrldev->rpdev = rpdev。
  2. device_initialize + set parent/class，cdev_init(&ctrldev->cdev, &rpmsg_ctrldev_fops)。
  3. twice ida_simple_get: from rpmsg_minor_ida get the secondary device number, from rpmsg_ctrl_ida get id (for rpmsg_ctrl%d）。
  4. cdev_device_add — register cdev and device together,/dev/rpmsg_ctrlN appear.
  5. Key point:dev->release = rpmsg_ctrldev_release_device is in cdev_device_addonly assign afterwards.
