Here choose DeepSeek because its tokens are relatively cheap and no external network connection is needed. Click the link below to apply for an API key.

DeepSeek Open Platform

https://platform.deepseek.com/api_keys

Editor Integration

Vscode

Download plugin:Claude Code for VS Code Anthropic anthropic.com, then find in settings@ext:Anthropic.claude-codeunderClaudeCode: Environment VariablesClickEdit in settings.json

Refer to the official documentation to set environment variables

AccessClaude Code

https://api-docs.deepseek.com/zh-cn/quick_start/agent_integrations/claude_code

as follows:

After that, open the Claude Code plugin in VSCode and it can be used.

You can also set environment variables in Windows PowerShell. First, open PowerShell with administrator privileges, then

Then write the environment variables from the official documentation, so that these environment variables are loaded every time PowerShell is opened.

Emacs

Emacs is generally used in Linux environments. Windows Emacs has too many bugs, so the following configuration is all under Linux. First, still install Claude Code, then set environment variables, $HOME/.bashrcor$HOME/.zshrc

Then enable the environment variables

Mainly use the claude-code.el package:

claude-code.el

https://github.com/stevemolitor/claude-code.el

The configuration is as follows:

InputC-c c cThen you can start the claude-code window. Other commands can be viewed via claude-code-transient (C-c c m).

Skill

Skill is an extension mechanism for Claude Code launched by Anthropic in October 2025. It can be understood as a skill pack for Claude Code, essentially a Markdown file.SKILL.mdIt contains the purpose, prompts, rules, and workflow of this Skill. Its storage location:

  • Project-level directorythat is, under your project root directory ${workspaceFolder}/.claude/skills/Skills placed here only take effect in the current project, suitable for skills strongly related to the project, such as the project-specific code standards, deployment processes, etc.
  • User-level directory, which is under your home directory $HOME/.claude/skills/Skills placed here can be used in all projects, suitable for general skills, such as making PPTs, writing documents, formatting WeChat public accounts, etc., which are not related to specific projects.

Another concept is MCP (Model Context Protocol). MCP is a standard protocol that allows AI assistants to safely interact with the external world. For example, enabling AI to read local files on your computer, operate GitHub repositories, query databases, send Slack messages, control browsers, etc.

Install Skill as follows:

  1. Manually copySKILL.mdto the project-level directory or user-level directory
  2. Install plugins

Reference:

Discover and install pre-built plugins through the marketplace

https://code.claude.com/docs/zh-CN/discover-plugins

The official Anthropic marketplace (claude-plugins-official) is automatically available when starting Claude Code. Run /plugin and go to the Discover tab to browse available content, or in claude.com/plugins view the directory.

Recommended Skills to install
Karpathy-Inspired Claude Code Guidelines:

Karpathy-Inspired Claude Code Guidelines

https://github.com/multica-ai/andrej-karpathy-skills

codegraph:

codegraph

https://github.com/colbymchenry/codegraph

draw.io

draw.io

https://github.com/jgraph/drawio-mcp/blob/main/plugins/claude-code/README.md

CC-Switch

A very useful AI aggregation tool that allows easy switching between different Agents and Models

CC-Switch

https://github.com/farion1231/cc-switch

Using CC-Switch eliminates the need to set environment variables

Reference

[1]
Step-by-step tutorial！Hands-on guide toClaude Codeinstall and use inSKILL
[2]
What experts are secretly using Claude Code Official plugin list
entry.summary: This article introduces the download, installation, and editor integration methods of Claude Code, detailing how to configure and use it with the DeepSeek API in VSCode and Emacs. At the same time, this article discusses Claude Code's Skill extension mechanism, MCP model context protocol, as well as the installation of related Skills and recommended plugins.

entry.title: smatch
entry.content:
timeline

timeline

2026-05-21

init

This article introduces Smatch, a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel. It details its compilation method, database construction process, and its ability to discover hidden bugs through cross-file analysis. Additionally, it summarizes the specific steps for running Smatch for static analysis on kernel and non-kernel projects, as well as the configuration methods for related script parameters.

Smatch is a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel. It can deeply trace code execution paths and discover deeply hidden bugs, such as null pointer dereferences, lock usage errors, resource leaks, etc. Reference documentation:

smatch

https://github.com/error27/smatch/blob/master/Documentation/smatch.rst

Compile smatch

kernel

Smatch essentially uses the kernel build system, so it must be able to correctly find the compiler and header files. Therefore, if cross-compilation is needed, environment variables must be set first.

Analyze and build database

build_kernel_data.sh Will append --call-tree, --info, --spammy, --data=$DATA_DIR Parameter invocation smatch_scripts/test_kernel.sh, then call smatch_data/db/create_db.sh Build smatch database

Smatch does not require building a database, but building one makes it more powerful. Once the total database is built, the next time you compile, Smatch can look up tables to know ‘how many times the printk function is called throughout the kernel’ or ‘whether a certain structure pointer has been freed in other C files’, thus achieving epic cross-file vulnerability detection.

Run smatch for static analysis on the kernel: Run smatch to check the entire kernel

Script parameters

  • --endian: Enable big/little endian byte order check.
  • --target "bzImage": Specify compilation target (default is bzImage modules).
  • --log smatch_compile.warns: Specify compilation log output file
  • --wlog smatch_warns.txt: Customize the output file name for Smatch warnings.

After compilation, two files will be generated in the current directory:

  • smatch_compile.warns: Complete kernel compilation log (including errors and warnings).
  • smatch_warns.txt: Pure Smatch static check warning information.

You can also check only the code under a specific file or folder

The smatch author recommends frequently updating the smatch database, because each update makes cross-function checks more accurate.

smatch reads some rules set in smatch_data at runtime. In ~/repository/linux/smatch-1.74/smatch.c in static char *get_data_dir(char *arg0) The function has related logic: you can pass --data=/path/to/smatch_data to specify the data directory.

out-of-tree module

For non-kernel projects, use the following method to build the database and analyze

  • --file-output Output one result per file
  • --succeed Indicates to continue analysis even if errors occur
  • --call-tree Indicates to enable cross-function call analysis
  • --info Indicates more detailed analysis logs
  • --spammy Indicates more aggressive checks

When using the --file-output parameter, smatch by default does not print warnings directly to the terminal’s stdout/stderr, but instead generates a bunch of hidden text files in the same directory as your code:

  • .hello_world.c.smatch (regular warnings)
  • .hello_world.c.smatch.caller_info (if you added --info and other parameters)
    Static check analysis

References

[1]
Automatic detection Linux Wrong tool - Static section
[2]
Kernel Testing Guide
entry.summary: This article introduces Smatch, a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel. It details its compilation method, database construction process, and its ability to discover hidden bugs through cross-file analysis. Additionally, it summarizes the specific steps for running Smatch for static analysis on kernel and non-kernel projects, as well as the configuration methods for related script parameters.

entry.title: LeetCode Hot 100 P1143 Longest Common Subsequence
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-03-21

init

Dynamic Programming

Problem:

P1143 Longest Common Subsequence

https://leetcode.cn/problems/longest-common-subsequence/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=top-100-liked

This is a classic problem,dp[i][j] Represents text1[0..=i] and text2[0..=j] the length of the longest common subsequence, the state transition equation is

  • When text1[i]==text2[j] text1[i] == text2[j] text1[i]==text2[j]:
    • dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1 dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1
  • otherwise:
    • dp[i][j]=max(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1]) dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) dp[i][j]=max(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1])
entry.summary: Dynamic Programming

entry.title: LeetCode Top 100 P416 Partition Equal Subset Sum
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-03-21

init

Dynamic Programming

Problem:

P416 Partition Equal Subset Sum

https://leetcode.cn/problems/partition-equal-subset-sum/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=top-100-liked

Sum the subarray, then divide the sum by 2, transforming the problem into finding a subset of the array that equals half of the total sum, which is the 0-1 knapsack problem

dp[i][j] represents whether there exists a selection scheme from the index range [0, i] of the array (selecting several positive integers, which can be 0) such that the sum of the selected positive integers equals j. Initially, all elements in dp are false.

Initialization:

  • If no positive integers are selected, the sum of the selected positive integers equals 0. Therefore, for all 0 ≤ i < n, dp[i][0] = true.
  • When i == 0, only one positive integer nums[0] can be selected, so dp[0][nums[0]] = true.

State transition:

  • dp[i][j] = dp[i−1][j] ∣ dp[i−1][j−nums[i]] (j >= nums[i])
  • dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (j < nums[i])
entry.summary: Dynamic Programming
